# Transcrição: 3_Situations_When_You_Should_NEVER_Tell_Stories

- **Fonte:** 3_Situations_When_You_Should_NEVER_Tell_Stories

- **Idioma:** en

## [00:00 - 03:02]

How do I decide when to tell a story and when not to tell a story? There's a few things to consider here. The first thing you have to ensure is, is my story relevant to the audience? I'll give you a very real example of this. One of my cousins was going through a really difficult time trying to have a child. And I remember we would gather with our friends and we would sit down and I've got some friends who are just again, not, they don't have good situation or awareness and there's no fault of their own. They're just very happy with where their lives are at. And they, they've got multiple kids, right? So they keep talking about their kids. Every story they bring up would be about their kids. And those stories, again, while they were beautiful, I think one or two of those stories would have been great, but the whole night they told probably 20 stories, 10 stories about their kids. And I could see it slowly wear one of my friends down. Right. And in those moments, you've got to be very sensitive because the stories you're sharing, they may not be relevant and B, they may actually be causing others pain. So that again, that situational awareness is really important. Again, bringing it back to you must know your audience. So again, if I know my audience, I just have to do a quick check. The story I'm about to share is relevant. Hello. Sorry to disrupt you just for a moment, but if you're interested in improving your communication skills and you're new to my content and you find these types of videos really useful, I've actually got a three part video series that helps you kickstart your communication skills journey. So if you want to check that out, that is just in the link in the description. Go check it out. The second thing that I would really check is cultural appropriateness. Just make sure you understand the audience you're speaking to and the culture that they're from and make sure that it's OK for you to talk about those topics. So again, that just requires you to have a little bit of knowledge about the culture. The third thing is be really sensitive to how much time you have, because if you've got six minutes to present, I would advise against telling a story. But if you've got at least 15 minutes or more, I would 100 percent invest at least a minute or two in sharing a story. Why? Because storytelling is all about connection. I think any form of communication, you must form a connection first before you share anything else, because until people know you care, they don't care. I'll add one more bonus thing to you here as well. And again, it comes back to this a lot. It comes back to a lot of EQ, which is emotional intelligence. And as you improve your emotional intelligence, you'll have more situational awareness. But the setting that you're moving into, is it a relaxed setting or is it a tense setting? Anytime I'm moving into a situation where it feels really tense, I might choose not to share a story. Whereas if the setting is relaxed and everyone's kind of chilled and you can tell by their body language, everyone's leaning back, everyone's having a good time, it's relaxed, then 100 percent I'm sharing a story. But if everyone's really tense and uptight, I most likely won't. I'll get straight to the point. So it does require a little bit of that situational awareness there.
